summary: Adversarial impl-review of 1.10.0-RC2 offline queue before cut: 4 lenses + skeptic verify + synthesis
logs: Confirmed (un-refuted) findings: 10; lenses reporting clean: security

survived.title: drain-batch-size is configured + documented but never honored — drain() reads and deletes the ENTIRE stream, contradicting spec §7
survived.scenario: Spec §7 defines `drain-batch-size` (default 100) as 'Max messages drained per connect (rest drained on next read loop / subsequent connect)', and release-notes-1.10.0.md:222 + api-guide.md:814 promise 'Max messages drained + delivered per connect'. A user enables offline, a user accumulates 5000 offline messages (within MAXLEN ~1000... or with a higher max-messages-per-user), then reconnects. The implementation drains and delivers ALL of them in one shot on a single connect, not 100. RedisOfflineQueueStore.drain() does `connection.sync().xrange(streamKey, Range.unbounded())` (the WHOLE stream `- +`), ClusterSessionHookImpl.drainOnConnect() iterates ALL returned messages and delete()s ALL their ids. The `drainBatchSize` property is never read by any code: it is defined in ClusterProperties.Offline (line 361 `private int drainBatchSize = 100;`) and the configure passes only maxMessagesPerUser/ttlSeconds/drainLockMs to the store constructor (NettyWebSocketClusterConfigure.java:561-563) — getDrainBatchSize() has ZERO callers in src/main, and neither RedisOfflineQueueStore nor InMemoryOfflineQueueStore takes or applies a batch limit. No test (RedisOfflineQueueStoreTest/IntegrationTest/OfflineDrainOnConnectTest) asserts batching. A documented, defaulted knob silently does nothing, and the per-connect bound the docs promise does not exist (large backlogs are delivered in one burst, not paced).
survived.evidence: RedisOfflineQueueStore.drain(): `List<StreamMessage<String, String>> entries = connection.sync().xrange(streamKey, Range.unbounded());` (reads the whole stream, no COUNT/limit). ClusterProperties.Offline: `private int drainBatchSize = 100;` with getter `getDrainBatchSize()`. Configure store wiring: `new RedisOfflineQueueStore(connection, envelopeCodec, messageAuthenticator, properties.getNodeId(), o.getMaxMessagesPerUser(), o.getTtlSeconds(), o.getDrainLockMs())` — getDrainBatchSize() is not passed. grep for drainBatchSize|getDrainBatchSize across src/main returns only ClusterProperties.java (definition + getter/setter), never a use-site.
survived.why:
CONFIRMED — could not refute. All claims verified against the actual code.

1. RedisOfflineQueueStore.drain() lines 142-143: `connection.sync().xrange(streamKey, Range.unbounded())` reads the WHOLE stream (- +), no COUNT/limit. The constructor (lines 98-100) accepts maxMessagesPerUser, ttlSeconds, drainLockMs — no batch-size parameter exists on the store at all.

2. ClusterProperties.Offline defines `private int drainBatchSize = 100;` (line 361) with getter `getDrainBatchSize()` (line 374). A project-wide grep for drainBatchSize|getDrainBatchSize returns ONLY ClusterProperties.java (def + getter/setter) and docs/metadata — ZERO use-sites in any src/main Java code.

3. NettyWebSocketClusterConfigure.java:561-563 wires `new RedisOfflineQueueStore(connection, envelopeCodec, messageAuthenticator, properties.getNodeId(), o.getMaxMessagesPerUser(), o.getTtlSeconds(), o.getDrainLockMs())` — getDrainBatchSize() is NOT passed. (Correct path is netty-websocket-cluster-spring-boot-starter/...; the finding's bare filename resolves there.)

4. The drain-on-connect consumer also applies no cap: ClusterSessionHookImpl.drainOnConnect() (lines 140-162) iterates `for (StoredMessage m : messages)` over the entire drained list, adds every id to deliveredIds, and delete()s ALL of them. No truncation to drainBatchSize anywhere.

5. The bound is promised in three places: spec §7 line 203 ('Max messages drained per connect (rest drained on next read loop / subsequent connect)'), release-notes-1.10.0.md:222 and api-guide.md:814 ('Max messages drained + delivered per connect'). The code delivers the whole backlog in one burst per connect — contradicting all three.

No guard/lock/ordering prevents this: the drain lock (SET NX) only serializes multi-device drains; it does not bound batch size. The TTL check only skips expired entries. Nothing paces delivery to 100.

This is a real, demonstrable spec-compliance defect (a documented, defaulted knob that is completely inert + a false per-connect-bound promise in 3 docs), not a hypothetical.

Severity note: the finding's '5000 messages' is slightly overstated — the per-user stream is bounded by MAXLEN ~ max-messages-per-user (default 1000, approximate trim), so the burst is capped in practice by retention, not unbounded. No data loss, crash, or security impact. MAJOR is appropriate (shipped defaulted config option does nothing + broken documented contract), not BLOCKER.

survived.title: offline.dropped_retention meter is wired into the binder but is incremented NOWHERE — the 'bounded-gap honesty meter' is permanently zero
survived.scenario: Spec §7 lists `dropped_retention` as a meter and instructs operators to 'alert on rate(...dropped_retention[5m]) > 0' (release-notes-1.10.0.md:230 repeats this as 'the bounded-gap honesty meter'). The meter `netty.cluster.offline.dropped_retention` is registered (NettyClusterMeterBinder.java:122). But the counter `offlineDroppedRetention` is never incremented anywhere in src/main: `addOfflineDroppedRetention(long)` has zero callers (grep across src/main returns only the definition, the mutator, and the getter). The two real retention-drop events both fail to count it: (1) MAXLEN `~` trim is done by Redis inside XADD (RedisOfflineQueueStore.enqueue line 121 `XAddArgs.Builder.maxlen(maxMessagesPerUser).approximateTrimming()`) — Redis silently evicts the oldest entries and returns nothing the app can count; (2) the lazy TTL drop in drain() (lines 153-158) does `continue` on an expired entry WITHOUT calling addOfflineDroppedRetention. So the alert the docs tell operators to set can NEVER fire — a user losing messages to MAXLEN/TTL trim sees the honesty meter flat at 0. The honesty meter is dishonest.
survived.evidence: ClusterRuntimeStats: `public void addOfflineDroppedRetention(long n) { offlineDroppedRetention.addAndGet(n); }` — no callers in src/main. RedisOfflineQueueStore TTL-drop: `if (entryMs != null && (now - entryMs) > ttlMillis) { log.debug("Dropping TTL-expired offline entry {} for user {}", id, userId); continue; }` (no counter bump). MeterBinder: `counter(registry, "netty.cluster.offline.dropped_retention", stats, ClusterRuntimeStats::getOfflineDroppedRetention, "...the bounded-gap honesty meter");`
survived.why:
VERIFIED REAL. All three cited facts confirmed against the actual code on this branch.

(1) Meter registered but no incrementer. NettyClusterMeterBinder.java:122-124 (in module netty-websocket-cluster-spring-boot-starter) registers counter "netty.cluster.offline.dropped_retention" bound to ClusterRuntimeStats::getOfflineDroppedRetention with description "...the bounded-gap honesty meter". The mutator ClusterRuntimeStats.java:146 `public void addOfflineDroppedRetention(long n) { offlineDroppedRetention.addAndGet(n); }` has ZERO callers: a grep for `addOfflineDroppedRetention` across all `src/main/**/*.java` in the repo returns only the field decl (line 112), the mutator (146), and the getter (156). No production code path bumps it.

(2) MAXLEN trim path cannot count. RedisOfflineQueueStore.java:120-122 enqueue() does `xadd(streamKey, XAddArgs.Builder.maxlen(maxMessagesPerUser).approximateTrimming(), ...)`. Redis evicts oldest entries server-side inside XADD and returns only the new entry id — the app has no eviction count and never calls the counter.

(3) TTL drop path doesn't count. RedisOfflineQueueStore.java:153-158 drain() detects `(now - entryMs) > ttlMillis`, logs `log.debug("Dropping TTL-expired offline entry ...")` and `continue`s with no counter bump. The class holds no ClusterRuntimeStats reference at all (no stats field/constructor param), so it structurally cannot increment the meter from here. The drain() caller (session hook) also never references the mutator (grep result), so no read-vs-delivered delta is computed upstream either.

No guard, lock, or ordering the finder missed — the meter is permanently pinned at 0.

SPEC IMPACT (spec-compliance lens): §7 (spec line 206-209) lists `dropped_retention` as a first-class meter, names it "the bounded-gap honesty meter", and instructs operators to "alert on rate(...dropped_retention[5m]) > 0". §6 (line 170-172) promises at-least-once-within-retention and states beyond-retention trim is "Metered via offline.dropped_retention." The code cannot honor that contract: any user losing messages to MAXLEN/TTL trim leaves the meter flat at 0, so the documented operator alert can never fire and the stated honesty mechanism is broken.

SEVERITY: MAJOR is correct. The feature still functions (not BLOCKER — no crash, no data corruption; the trim itself is by-design bounded loss), but the documented observability/honesty contract on the loss path is unmet, defeating the exact alert the spec/release notes tell operators to configure (not MINOR — this is a load-bearing, explicitly-specified honesty meter, not cosmetic).

survived.title: drain() inline comment falsely claims TTL-expired (and HMAC-rejected / decode-failed) entries are XDEL'd 'alongside the delivered ids' — they are never deleted
survived.line: 153-177 (skip-via-continue) vs ClusterSessionHookImpl.java:145-153 (deliveredIds only from returned messages)
survived.scenario: drain() skips three classes of entry with `continue` and never adds their id to the returned list: TTL-expired (line 156-158), missing-field/HMAC-rejected (line 164-166), and codec-decode-failed (line 173-176). The comment at line 156-157 asserts a TTL-skipped entry 'will be XDELed alongside the delivered ids — see delete()'s caller passing all read ids'. But the caller (ClusterSessionHookImpl.drainOnConnect) builds deliveredIds ONLY from the StoredMessages drain() actually RETURNS (`for (StoredMessage m : messages) { ...; deliveredIds.add(m.getId()); }`) — the skipped ids are not in `messages`, so they are NOT passed to delete() and are NOT XDEL'd. A poison entry (un-decodable or failing HMAC) at the head of the stream is therefore re-read and re-skipped on every single drain forever, until MAXLEN eventually evicts it. Functionally bounded by MAXLEN (not a leak), but the code comment is factually wrong and the poison entry wastes one decode attempt per connect indefinitely.
survived.evidence: RedisOfflineQueueStore.drain() comment: `// (it will be XDELed alongside the delivered ids — see delete()'s caller passing all read ids).` followed by `continue;`. The caller never passes skipped ids: `for (StoredMessage m : messages) { ... deliveredIds.add(m.getId()); }` builds deliveredIds purely from the returned (non-skipped) set.
survived.why:
I could not refute this finding; the code matches the finder's description exactly.

CITED CODE CONFIRMED:
- RedisOfflineQueueStore.drain() (lines 149-178) has three skip-via-continue paths that do NOT add the entry id to the returned list `out`:
  1. TTL-expired (lines 153-158): `if (entryMs != null && (now - entryMs) > ttlMillis) { log.debug(...); continue; }`
  2. Missing-field / HMAC-rejected (lines 164-166): `if (data == null) { log.warn(...); continue; }`
  3. Codec-decode failure (lines 168-177): `decode` returns null → only a warn (no add); `decode` throws → caught, warn, no add.
  So `out` contains ONLY successfully-decoded `StoredMessage`s.

- The disputed comment at lines 151-152: `// Lazy TTL drop: ... skip it // (it will be XDELed alongside the delivered ids — see delete()'s caller passing all read ids).`

CALLER CONFIRMED (ClusterSessionHookImpl.drainOnConnect, lines 145-154): `deliveredIds` is built purely from the RETURNED list — `for (StoredMessage m : messages) { ...; deliveredIds.add(m.getId()); }` — then `offlineStore.delete(userId, deliveredIds)`. The skipped/continue'd ids are never in `messages`, hence never in `deliveredIds`, hence never passed to delete(), hence never XDEL'd. The comment's claim that the caller passes "all read ids" is factually false — it passes only the non-skipped (returned) ids.

CONSEQUENCE VERIFIED: enqueue() trims via `XAddArgs.Builder.maxlen(...).approximateTrimming()` (MAXLEN only); there is no XTRIM MINID / time-based trim and no XDEL of skipped ids anywhere. So a TTL-expired or poison (un-decodable / HMAC-rejected) entry at the stream head is re-read and re-skipped on every drain, wasting one unwrap/decode attempt per connect, until MAXLEN eviction removes it. Functionally bounded by MAXLEN (not an unbounded leak), but the inline comment is demonstrably wrong about TTL-expired (and HMAC-rejected / decode-failed) entries being XDEL'd.

This is a real, non-hypothetical defect in the actual code, demonstrable purely by reading the two files. SEVERITY MINOR is correct: it is a false code comment plus a small bounded inefficiency, with no correctness/data-loss impact (at-least-once is preserved; the queue stays bounded).

survived.title: Drain lock leaks on every empty-queue (and TTL/HMAC-only) connect — held until PX expiry, can swallow a real message on a concurrent device reconnect
survived.line: 142-143 (caller) + RedisOfflineQueueStore.java:144-146 (drain returns empty while still holding the lock)
survived.scenario: The drain lock is released ONLY inside delete() (RedisOfflineQueueStore.delete L191 DELs the lock). drain() acquires the SET NX PX lock BEFORE XRANGE (correct), but when the stream is empty it returns Collections.emptyList() at L144-146 WITHOUT releasing — the lock stays held. drainOnConnect then hits `if (messages == null || messages.isEmpty()) return;` (L142-143) and NEVER calls delete(), so the lock is not released and survives until drain-lock-ms (default 5000ms) auto-expiry. This fires on the overwhelmingly common path: every connect of a user with no queued messages leaves netty:offline-lock:{b64userId} held for 5s. Concrete loss window: user's phone reconnects with an empty queue at t=0 (lock held to t=5s); at t=1s a sender enqueues a real message; at t=2s the user's laptop reconnects — its drain() SET NX fails (stale lock still held), returns empty, so the just-queued message is NOT backfilled on this connect and is only delivered on a later reconnect after the stale lock expires. Same leak occurs when the only entries are TTL-expired or HMAC-rejected (drain builds an empty `out` while holding the lock). Must-fix #2 says the lock is released 'only AFTER delivered ids are XDEL'd' — but on the empty path nothing is XDEL'd and nothing releases it.
survived.evidence: ClusterSessionHookImpl.drainOnConnect: `if (messages == null || messages.isEmpty()) { return; // nothing queued, or another device is draining }` — no delete(). RedisOfflineQueueStore.drain: `if (entries == null || entries.isEmpty()) { return Collections.<StoredMessage>emptyList(); }` after `set(lockKey, nodeId, SetArgs.Builder.nx().px(drainLockMs))` returned OK. delete() is the only DEL of the lock: `connection.sync().del(lockKey(userId));`
survived.why:
CONFIRMED as a real defect in the actual code (note: the file is at .../cluster/room/RedisOfflineQueueStore.java, not the path in the finding's title, but the cited lines/behavior match exactly).

Verified mechanics:
1. EMPTY-QUEUE LOCK LEAK (real): RedisOfflineQueueStore.drain() acquires the per-userId lock at L134-135 (`connection.sync().set(lockKey, nodeId, SetArgs.Builder.nx().px(drainLockMs))`), then at L144-146 returns `Collections.emptyList()` on an empty stream WITHOUT releasing it. The ONLY lock release is delete()'s `connection.sync().del(lockKey(userId))` at L191. The caller ClusterSessionHookImpl.drainOnConnect (L141-143) returns on `messages == null || messages.isEmpty()` WITHOUT ever calling delete(). So on every empty-queue connect the lock netty:offline-lock:{b64userId} is orphaned until PX auto-expiry (default 5000ms). The same leak hits the TTL-expired-only / HMAC-rejected-only / decode-failure-only paths: those `continue` past every entry (L153-177), build an empty `out`, and return it at L179 while still holding the lock. This is the common path (most reconnects have nothing queued).

2. NO SAFETY NET / NO RE-DRAIN: grep confirms drain() is invoked ONLY from drainOnConnect, exactly once per connect via bindUser().thenRun() — there is no background/scheduled re-drain loop. So a message enqueued during the stale-lock window, followed by a concurrent device reconnect whose drain() SET NX fails (L136-139 → returns empty), is NOT backfilled on that connect.

3. CROSS-CONFIRMED at SPI/design level: the test-stub InMemoryOfflineQueueStore.drain() has the identical leak (L56 `drainLocks.add`, returns empty snapshot at L68 without removing the lock; only delete()/removeAllForUser remove it) — and with no TTL it would leak forever. Existing tests (InMemoryOfflineQueueStoreTest.removeAllForUserClearsQueueAndLock L81-82; RedisOfflineQueueStoreIntegrationTest.removeAllForUserClearsStreamAndLock L156 "hold the lock") confirm drain() never self-releases. The design-review note explicitly specified the intended lifecycle as "drain() acquires before XRANGE and releases after delete()" — the empty path violates that.

SEVERITY CORRECTION (BLOCKER → MAJOR): The finding's framing of "swallow a real message" overstates impact. The message is NOT permanently lost — it is never XDEL'd, so it stays in the Redis stream and IS delivered on a subsequent reconnect once the lock auto-expires (drain-lock-ms, default 5s, bounds the window). The spec contract is at-least-once within the retention window with FIFO backfill on reconnect; a delayed-but-retained delivery still falls inside at-least-once. So the true impact is (a) a missed/delayed backfill on a concurrent multi-device reconnect bounded by ~5s, and (b) lock-contention churn on the common empty-connect path — a genuine concurrency defect violating the intended lock lifecycle, but not permanent data loss and self-healing via PX expiry. That is MAJOR, not BLOCKER.

The finder's one inaccuracy worth noting: their Must-fix-#2 framing implies the lock should release only after XDEL, but the actual contract bug is that the empty/all-skipped paths release NOTHING and the caller never reaches delete() — the fix is to release the lock on the empty/all-skipped return (or have the caller always ack/unlock).

survived.title: delete() unconditionally DELs the drain lock without verifying ownership — can delete a different device's freshly-acquired lock (Redlock release-foreign-lock bug)
survived.scenario: drain() sets the lock with PX drain-lock-ms (default 5000ms) holding value=nodeId. The whole drain→deliver→delete sequence (XRANGE of the entire stream, then N WebSocket frame deliveries across the local sender, then delete) is NOT bounded by drain-lock-ms. If delivering a large drained batch (drain reads the WHOLE stream unbounded — see the drain-batch-size finding) exceeds 5s, the lock auto-expires; device B then reconnects and acquires a NEW lock (its own nodeId). Device A's delete() finally runs and executes `connection.sync().del(lockKey(userId))` UNCONDITIONALLY — releasing device B's lock mid-drain. Device B's stream read and a third concurrent drainer can then both proceed → double delivery, defeating the exactly-once guarantee the lock exists to provide. A correct release must be a check-and-del (GET value == nodeId, or Lua compare-and-DEL), as the project already does for its other SET-NX leader locks.
survived.evidence: delete(): `connection.sync().del(lockKey(userId));  // Release the drain lock regardless` — no value check. Acquire side stores ownership: `connection.sync().set(lockKey, nodeId, SetArgs.Builder.nx().px(drainLockMs))`. Javadoc claims 'so a crashed drainer can't wedge a user's queue' via PX expiry, but the same PX expiry is what enables the foreign-lock delete.
survived.why:
CONFIRMED — the cited code is exactly as described and the defect is real. At RedisOfflineQueueStore.java:191, delete() does `connection.sync().del(lockKey(userId));` (comment: "// Release the drain lock regardless") — an UNCONDITIONAL DEL with no value/ownership check and no Lua compare-and-del. The lock is acquired at lines 134-135 with `SET lockKey nodeId NX PX drainLockMs`, storing the owner's nodeId as a token — but that token is written on acquire and NEVER read on release. This is the textbook Redlock "release-foreign-lock" anti-pattern.

The failure scenario is reachable in THIS code, not hypothetical:
1. The drain read is genuinely UNBOUNDED: drain() at line 143 uses `Range.unbounded()` and reads up to maxMessagesPerUser (default 1000) entries. The `drain-batch-size` config (default 100, documented as "Max messages drained per connect") is a real property in ClusterProperties but is NEVER plumbed into the RedisOfflineQueueStore constructor (it takes only maxMessagesPerUser/ttlSeconds/drainLockMs — verified in auto-config NettyWebSocketClusterConfigure L561-563) and is never applied to the XRANGE — so it is silently ignored, leaving the batch unbounded.
2. The whole drain->deliver->delete chain is NOT bounded by, and does not renew, the lock TTL. The caller (ClusterSessionHookImpl.drainOnConnect, L146-157) iterates ALL drained messages calling clusterSender.deliverOfflineMessage -> localSender.sendMessage synchronously per message BEFORE delete() runs.
3. If that chain exceeds drainLockMs (default 5000ms) — e.g. a large drained batch over a slow/backpressured channel, a GC pause, or a stalled XDEL — device A's lock auto-expires, device B reconnects and SET-NX acquires a NEW lock (value=node-B), then device A's delete() runs and unconditionally DELs node-B's freshly-acquired lock. A third concurrent drainer can then enter while B is still draining -> double delivery, defeating the exactly-once guarantee the lock exists to provide (spec §6: "Multi-device double-delivery is prevented by the per-userId drain lock... Without the lock this would be a *deterministic* duplicate").

Existing tests do not cover this: MultiDeviceDrainLockTest exercises only the happy path where the holder finishes well within 5s, so the regression gate would not catch an expiry-then-foreign-delete.

One factual overreach in the finding: it claims "the project already does check-and-del for its other SET-NX leader locks." That is FALSE — RedisClusterReaper (the SET-NX leader lock) relies purely on PX expiry and never explicitly releases the claim, so there is no check-and-del precedent. This mischaracterizes precedent but does NOT undermine the defect; the correct fix (GETDEL/Lua compare-and-DEL fencing) is still warranted.

SEVERITY: MAJOR is correct. It is a genuine lock-correctness defect that breaks the headline multi-device exactly-once guarantee, but it is gated behind a timing precondition (drain+deliver of a batch exceeding drainLockMs concurrent with a second-device reconnect) rather than failing unconditionally on every run — so it is below BLOCKER but clearly above MINOR. The unbounded-batch issue (ignored drain-batch-size) materially raises its probability.

survived.title: TTL-expired / HMAC-rejected entries are dropped from delivery but never XDEL'd — they leak in the stream forever (contradicts the in-code comment)
survived.scenario: drain() skips a TTL-expired entry with `continue` and NEVER adds its id to `out`. The caller (drainOnConnect) builds deliveredIds ONLY from the returned messages (`deliveredIds.add(m.getId())` for m in `out`), so the skipped ids are never passed to delete()/XDEL. The drain comment claims the opposite: 'it will be XDELed alongside the delivered ids — see delete()'s caller passing all read ids'. It does not. Effect: a stale (or HMAC-bad) entry is re-read on EVERY subsequent drain, re-evaluated as expired, re-skipped, forever — it is never removed. If a user has one stale entry and otherwise an empty queue, every connect drains an empty `out` (so delete() is also never called per finding #1), so the stream key and its dead entry persist indefinitely. The TTL-cleanup contract in spec §6 ('Beyond it → trimmed') silently does not hold for these entries.
survived.evidence: drain(): `if (entryMs != null && (now - entryMs) > ttlMillis) { log.debug("Dropping TTL-expired offline entry {} ..."); continue; }` and `if (data == null) { log.warn("Offline entry {} ... dropped ..."); continue; }` — neither records the id for deletion. Comment at L151-152: 'it will be XDELed alongside the delivered ids — see delete()'s caller passing all read ids'. Caller only adds delivered ids: `deliveredIds.add(m.getId());` inside the loop over the returned `messages`.
survived.why:
CONFIRMED real defect, but severity overstated (MAJOR → MINOR).

Every link in the finding's chain is verified in the actual code:

1. RedisOfflineQueueStore.drain() (file: .../room/RedisOfflineQueueStore.java):
   - TTL-expired entry: lines 153-158, `if (entryMs != null && (now - entryMs) > ttlMillis) { log.debug(...); continue; }` — the id is NEVER added to `out`.
   - HMAC-rejected / missing-field entry: lines 160-166, `if (data == null) { log.warn("...dropped (no field, or rejected HMAC)"); continue; }` — id never added to `out`.
   - Only line 171, `out.add(new StoredMessage(id, env))`, records an id, and only for successfully unwrapped+decoded entries.

2. The in-code comment at lines 151-152 ("it will be XDELed alongside the delivered ids — see delete()'s caller passing all read ids") is factually FALSE.

3. Caller ClusterSessionHookImpl.drainOnConnect() (lines 140-167) builds `deliveredIds` ONLY from the returned `messages`: `for (StoredMessage m : messages) { ...; deliveredIds.add(m.getId()); }` (line 153), then `offlineStore.delete(userId, deliveredIds)` (line 157). Skipped TTL/HMAC ids are not in `messages`, so they never reach delete()/XDEL. The comment's claim that the caller passes "all read ids" is wrong — it passes only delivered ids.

4. No alternative removal path exists. Grep confirms the only XDEL is delete() (line 188), driven by messageIds. enqueue() (line 121) uses only `XAddArgs...maxlen(maxMessagesPerUser).approximateTrimming()` — MAXLEN, NOT MINID/time-based trim — so TTL removal depends ENTIRELY on the lazy drain check, which deletes nothing. No scheduled reaper. removeAllForUser() is a full-queue wipe used only on explicit user removal, not expired-entry cleanup.

5. The "empty out" interaction holds: drainOnConnect returns early at lines 142-143 (`if (messages == null || messages.isEmpty()) return;`) before building deliveredIds or calling delete(). So a user whose only queued entry is stale/forged yields an empty out → delete() never called → the entry is re-read, re-evaluated as expired/forged, re-skipped on EVERY subsequent connect, forever. MAXLEN cannot help because a 1-entry stream never exceeds the ~1000 cap.

So: the leak is real, demonstrable in this exact code (not hypothetical), it silently violates spec §6's TTL-trim contract for these entries, and the code comment is provably wrong.

SEVERITY CORRECTION (MAJOR → MINOR): the blast radius is bounded. Leaked dead entries are capped per user by `MAXLEN ~ maxMessagesPerUser` (approximate-trimming slack aside), so they cannot grow unboundedly. There is NO delivery-correctness impact: a stale/forged entry never blocks valid entries (valid ones are still decoded and delivered in the same drain), causes no double-delivery and no loss of valid messages. The cost is repeated re-decode/re-HMAC work on each drain plus a key/entries that never go away — a resource/cleanliness leak and a misleading comment, not a data-integrity or availability bug. That is MINOR, not MAJOR.

survived.title: Offline stream key has no Redis EXPIRE — a user who is enqueued-to but never reconnects leaks the key (and its messages) permanently
survived.scenario: enqueue() does XADD with MAXLEN ~ only; it never sets EXPIRE/PEXPIRE on netty:offline:{b64userId}. The only retention mechanisms are (a) MAXLEN approximate count trimming, which only caps the entry count of a still-growing stream and never empties it, and (b) the lazy ttl-seconds age check inside drain(), which requires a drain to ever happen. For a userId that receives offline messages but never reconnects (churned account, bot target, abandoned device, or simply a user who never comes back), the stream key lives forever in Redis with no age bound — directly violating spec §6/config 'ttl-seconds (default 7 days)... per-message age cap'. Over a large/churning user population this is unbounded Redis key growth. The TTL is only honored for users who reconnect; the spec presents it as an unconditional retention cap.
survived.evidence: enqueue(): `connection.async().xadd(streamKey, XAddArgs.Builder.maxlen(maxMessagesPerUser).approximateTrimming(), Collections.singletonMap(FIELD, data))` — no .expire()/PEXPIRE anywhere in the file (grep for expire/pexpire returns only the lock PX and the javadoc/log strings). ttlMillis is consulted only in drain().
survived.why:
CODE CLAIM VERIFIED. enqueue() (RedisOfflineQueueStore.java:117-125) does exactly `connection.async().xadd(streamKey, XAddArgs.Builder.maxlen(maxMessagesPerUser).approximateTrimming(), Collections.singletonMap(FIELD, data))`. I grepped the whole file for expire/pexpire: the ONLY hits are (a) a javadoc comment about the drain *lock* auto-expiring via SET NX PX (line 67) and (b) a log string in drain() (line 156). There is NO EXPIRE/PEXPIRE on the stream key `netty:offline:{b64userId}` anywhere. The only retention mechanisms are: MAXLEN~ approximate trimming (caps entry COUNT per stream, never empties/deletes the key) and the lazy ttlMillis age-check, which lives ONLY inside drain() (lines 153-159). removeAllForUser() and delete() only run on explicit invocation. I confirmed there is no background reaper for offline streams — the reconciliation/reaper machinery in the module (ClusterNodeManager, RedisClusterReaper) is for node heartbeats, not offline queues.

SCENARIO IS REAL AND DETERMINISTIC. For a userId that is enqueued-to but never reconnects (churned/abandoned account, bot target), drain() is never called → the lazy TTL drop never executes → delete()/removeAllForUser() never run → the key lives in Redis indefinitely. MAXLEN bounds each stream's entry count but not the number of keys, so over a churning user population this is unbounded Redis key-COUNT growth. No guard/lock/ordering prevents it; the finder did not miss anything in the code path.

WHERE THE FINDING OVERSTATES. The finding says this 'directly violates spec §6/config' and that the spec 'presents [ttl-seconds] as an unconditional retention cap.' That framing is inaccurate. The spec is explicit and self-consistent that ttl-seconds is a per-message age cap that is LAZY-CHECKED ON DRAIN, not a key-level Redis EXPIRE: §7 config table = 'Per-message age cap (7 days); lazy-checked on drain + stream trim'; §4 component table = 'per-message TTL via stream trim + a lazy age check on drain'; and ClusterProperties.java:358 javadoc = 'Per-message age cap (seconds); lazily dropped on drain + bounded by stream trim.' So the code does exactly what the spec describes. This is a design GAP/omission (the spec never addresses the never-reconnecting-user orphaned-key case) rather than a code-vs-spec contract violation.

NET: the defect itself — no key-level EXPIRE → orphaned offline-stream keys leak permanently for users who are enqueued-to but never reconnect — is a genuine, demonstrable property of the actual code, not a hypothetical. It survives. But it is NOT refuted-away by any guard.

SEVERITY: downgraded MAJOR→MINOR. Mitigating: (1) the entire offline feature is opt-in and default-OFF (offline.enable=false; ClusterProperties.java:353), so out-of-the-box behavior is unaffected; (2) MAXLEN~ bounds each individual stream's size, so per-key memory is bounded — it is key COUNT, not per-key size, that grows; (3) it is a slow operational/resource-growth concern over a long horizon, not data loss, corruption, or a security/correctness failure; (4) the finding overstates the spec basis. A real but bounded operational leak on an opt-in path is MINOR, not MAJOR. A one-line `.expire()` on the stream key in enqueue (e.g., PEXPIRE = ttlMillis) would close it.

survived.title: drain-batch-size config is defined, documented, and metered as a contract but wired NOWHERE — drain reads the entire unbounded stream
survived.scenario: ClusterProperties.Offline.drainBatchSize=100 exists with getter/setter and is documented in spec §7 ('Max messages drained per connect (rest drained on next read loop / subsequent connect)'), api-guide.md, release-notes-1.10.0.md (EN+中文), and additional-spring-configuration-metadata.json. But RedisOfflineQueueStore's constructor does not even accept it (auto-config L561-563 passes only maxMessagesPerUser/ttlSeconds/drainLockMs), and drain() does `xrange(streamKey, Range.unbounded())` — it reads and returns the WHOLE stream (up to MAXLEN, default 1000) in one shot and the hook delivers all of them. A user with 1000 queued messages gets all 1000 read into a List<StoredMessage> on the offline executor and dispatched synchronously on connect — not the bounded 100 the operator configured. The knob is silently inert, and the documented backpressure ('rest drained on subsequent connect') does not exist. Either the impl must honor the batch (XRANGE COUNT / Range with limit and partial-ack) or the knob+docs must be removed; shipping a documented-but-dead config knob in an RC is a correctness/honesty gap.
survived.evidence: drain(): `List<StreamMessage<String, String>> entries = connection.sync().xrange(streamKey, Range.unbounded());` — no COUNT/limit. ClusterProperties: `private int drainBatchSize = 100; ... public int getDrainBatchSize()` — grep confirms getDrainBatchSize() has zero call sites in main code. Auto-config offlineQueueStore bean constructs `new RedisOfflineQueueStore(connection, envelopeCodec, messageAuthenticator, properties.getNodeId(), o.getMaxMessagesPerUser(), o.getTtlSeconds(), o.getDrainLockMs())` — drainBatchSize not passed.
survived.why:
Finding is CONFIRMED in the actual code — I could not refute it.

(1) The knob exists and is a documented public contract: ClusterProperties.Offline.drainBatchSize=100 (ClusterProperties.java:361) with getter/setter (L374-375), documented in spec §7 (L203), api-guide.md (L814, L1083), release-notes-1.10.0.md EN+中文 (L222, L425), and additional-spring-configuration-metadata.json (L216-219, description: "Max messages drained + delivered per connect (the rest drain on a subsequent connect)").

(2) It is wired NOWHERE. RedisOfflineQueueStore has no batch field/param at all — grep for `batch` inside the file returns nothing; its constructor (L98-100) takes only maxMessagesPerUser/ttlSeconds/drainLockMs. drain() does `connection.sync().xrange(streamKey, Range.unbounded())` (L142-143) — reads the ENTIRE stream into a List<StoredMessage> with no COUNT/limit.

(3) Auto-config NettyWebSocketClusterConfigure.java:561-563 constructs the store passing only maxMessagesPerUser/ttlSeconds/drainLockMs — getDrainBatchSize() is not passed.

(4) getDrainBatchSize() has ZERO call sites in main code: repo-wide grep finds it only in ClusterProperties.java itself (definition + getter + setter). It is genuinely dead.

(5) No downstream guard re-applies the limit: ClusterSessionHookImpl.drainOnConnect (L140-166) iterates the FULL drained list with no subList/limit/cap, delivers each via clusterSender.deliverOfflineMessage, and acks all ids. So a user with up to max-messages-per-user (default 1000) queued messages gets all of them read and dispatched on a single connect — not the bounded 100 the operator configured. The documented "rest drain on a subsequent connect" backpressure does not exist.

This is a real defect in THIS code (a documented, bindable, public config property that is silently inert and contradicts the published docs/metadata in two languages), not a hypothetical. The failure scenario is deterministic, not race-dependent.

Two minor caveats that adjust framing but not the verdict: (a) the title says "unbounded stream" — strictly the read is bounded by the stream MAXLEN (~max-messages-per-user, default 1000), so it is not literally unbounded and there is no memory blowup beyond the configured retention cap; (b) "metered as a contract" is loose — drainBatchSize is documented and bindable but I found no metric keyed on it. Neither caveat negates the core defect: an inert documented config knob.

survived.title: offline.drain-batch-size config knob is documented + bound but NEVER applied — drain reads the whole stream unbounded (config-that-does-nothing, contradicts the project's own stated principle)
survived.scenario: Operator sets server.netty.websocket.cluster.offline.drain-batch-size=100 (or relies on the documented default 100) expecting at most 100 offline messages delivered per reconnect, with the rest drained on a subsequent connect. In reality drain() does `connection.sync().xrange(streamKey, Range.unbounded())` — it reads and delivers the ENTIRE stream (up to max-messages-per-user, default 1000) on a single connect. getDrainBatchSize() is never called anywhere in main code (grep finds it only in ClusterProperties getters/setters, docs, the metadata json, and the plan — zero functional read site). The RedisOfflineQueueStore constructor is invoked at NettyWebSocketClusterConfigure.java:561-563 WITHOUT passing drainBatchSize. Net effect: a reconnecting user with a full 1000-message backlog gets all 1000 delivered+XDEL'd in one drain, not 100. This is not a disabled-path regression (offline must be enabled to reach it) and causes over-delivery, not loss — hence MINOR — but it directly violates the project's own ClusterProperties javadoc principle: "config that does nothing is worse than no config."
survived.evidence: RedisOfflineQueueStore.drain(): `List<StreamMessage<String, String>> entries = connection.sync().xrange(streamKey, Range.unbounded());` — no LIMIT, no drainBatchSize. ClusterProperties.Offline: `private int drainBatchSize = 100; ... public int getDrainBatchSize() { return drainBatchSize; }` (getter has no caller). metadata: `"name": "server.netty.websocket.cluster.offline.drain-batch-size" ... "description": "Max messages drained + delivered per connect (the rest drain on a subsequent connect)."` — a promise the code does not keep.
survived.why:
CONFIRMED real defect — every cited location verified in the actual code:

1. RedisOfflineQueueStore.drain() (lines 142-143) reads the stream unbounded: `connection.sync().xrange(streamKey, Range.unbounded())`. Lettuce's two-arg xrange(key, Range) reads the entire range; the bounded form xrange(key, Range, Limit) is NOT used. No drainBatchSize, no LIMIT.

2. ClusterProperties.Offline declares `private int drainBatchSize = 100;` (line 361) with getter/setter at 374-375. The getter has no functional caller.

3. Neither RedisOfflineQueueStore constructor (lines 93-96 and 98-114) accepts a drainBatchSize parameter. The bean factory at NettyWebSocketClusterConfigure.java:561-563 passes only nodeId, maxMessagesPerUser, ttlSeconds, drainLockMs — the knob is never threaded in.

4. The sole production drain caller, ClusterSessionHookImpl.drainOnConnect (lines 141-154), iterates the full `messages` list with zero truncation (no subList, no batch cap), delivers all, then deletes all drained ids.

5. A project-wide grep for getDrainBatchSize/drainBatchSize/drain-batch-size finds it ONLY in the getter/setter, the metadata json, api-guide.md (814, 1083), release-notes-1.10.0.md (222, 425), and the spec/plan docs — never in a functional read site.

6. The metadata json:216-219 promises "Max messages drained + delivered per connect (the rest drain on a subsequent connect)" and api-guide.md:814 / release-notes promise the same bounded behavior. The code does not keep that promise.

No guard, lock, or ordering prevents the failing behavior; the configured value is simply ignored. Net effect: a reconnecting user with a backlog up to max-messages-per-user (default 1000) gets the ENTIRE backlog delivered + XDEL'd in one drain, not the documented 100. The scenario is reachable (offline.enable=true is required to reach this bean, but that is the intended production config for this feature) and reproducible — not hypothetical.

Severity: MINOR is correct. It is an unwired/dead config knob whose documentation makes a promise the code breaks ("config that does nothing"), but the consequence is over-delivery, not data loss or a security hole, and it is on an opt-in path. Note on the regression lens: this knob is new in RC2, so nothing regressed from a previously-working state; however the finding does not claim a behavioral regression — it claims an unapplied documented config, which is accurate and stands.

survived.title: userIdResolver bean is created on non-standalone transports (Redis-Cluster / all-NATS) while userRegistry+offlineStore are not — offline.enable=true silently no-ops there with an orphan resolver bean and a misleading startup log
survived.scenario: Deployment uses Redis Cluster mode (redis.cluster-nodes set) or all-NATS (nats.servers + nats.registry=true) AND sets offline.enable=true. The userIdResolver @Bean has only @ConditionalOnProperty(offline.enable=true) + @ConditionalOnMissingBean — it has NO @ConditionalOnExpression(STANDALONE_REDIS_REGISTRY) — so the default HandshakeUserIdResolver IS created. But userRegistry and offlineQueueStore carry STANDALONE_REDIS_REGISTRY and are NOT created (there are no Cluster/NATS variants of these two beans, unlike SessionRegistry which has 3). Result: clusterMessageSender() and clusterSessionHook() both compute offlineEnabled = (... && userRegistry != null && offlineQueueStore != null) = false, so sendToUser() throws IllegalState and the hook uses the emptyMap path — i.e. offline is silently OFF despite offline.enable=true, leaving an orphan resolver bean instantiated for nothing. The HandshakeUserIdResolver constructor also emits its loud 'CONVENIENCE/TESTING ONLY' WARN at startup even though the offline path is inert, which is misleading. No NPE, no crash, and spec §9 does say 'Redis-only RC2' so the inert behavior is in-scope — but the resolver bean should carry the same transport gate (or fail fast) so offline.enable=true on a non-standalone transport doesn't quietly do nothing.
survived.evidence: userIdResolver: `@Bean @ConditionalOnProperty(prefix = "...offline", name = "enable", havingValue = "true") @ConditionalOnMissingBean(UserIdResolver.class)` — note the ABSENT @ConditionalOnExpression(STANDALONE_REDIS_REGISTRY). userRegistry: `@Bean(destroyMethod = "shutdown") @ConditionalOnExpression(STANDALONE_REDIS_REGISTRY) @ConditionalOnProperty(...offline... enable=true) @ConditionalOnMissingBean(UserRegistry.class)`. clusterSessionHook(): `boolean offlineEnabled = properties.getOffline().isEnable() && userIdResolver != null && userRegistry != null && offlineQueueStore != null;`
survived.why:
VERIFIED REAL (MINOR). Every load-bearing claim matches the actual code in NettyWebSocketClusterConfigure.java.

(1) userIdResolver @Bean (lines 528-535) carries ONLY @ConditionalOnProperty(server.netty.websocket.cluster.offline.enable=true) + @ConditionalOnMissingBean(UserIdResolver.class). It has NO @ConditionalOnExpression(STANDALONE_REDIS_REGISTRY) transport gate. Confirmed by reading lines 528-535.

(2) userRegistry (538-547) and offlineQueueStore (550-564) BOTH carry @ConditionalOnExpression(STANDALONE_REDIS_REGISTRY) in addition to the offline.enable gate. A whole-module grep confirms these are the ONLY definitions of UserRegistry/OfflineQueueStore beans — there are no Cluster-mode or all-NATS variants (unlike SessionRegistry which has 3 transport variants).

(3) SpEL gate semantics (lines 100-128): STANDALONE_REDIS_REGISTRY = STANDALONE_TRANSPORT('cluster-nodes'.trim().isEmpty()) AND NOT_ALL_NATS. In Redis-Cluster mode (cluster-nodes non-empty) STANDALONE_TRANSPORT is false → gate false. In all-NATS mode (nats.servers set + nats.registry=true) NOT_ALL_NATS is false → gate false. In BOTH cases registry+store beans are suppressed, but the ungated resolver IS still created when offline.enable=true. The scenario is real, not hypothetical.

(4) Consumer behavior confirmed: clusterSessionHook() (lines 781-782) computes offlineEnabled = offline.isEnable() && userIdResolver!=null && userRegistry!=null && offlineQueueStore!=null → false when registry/store are absent → hook uses the disabled/emptyMap path. clusterMessageSender() (lines 744-748) only wires the setters when non-null; ClusterMessageSender.isOfflineEnabled() returns userRegistry!=null && offlineStore!=null (lines 255-257) → false; sendToUser() then throws IllegalStateException("User-addressed delivery is disabled...") at lines 808-810. So offline.enable=true silently no-ops on these transports.

(5) Misleading log confirmed: HandshakeUserIdResolver constructor (lines 86-89) unconditionally logs the loud "CONVENIENCE/TESTING ONLY ... NO authentication" WARN at startup, firing even though the offline path is inert on Redis-Cluster/all-NATS — exactly the misleading-startup-log claim.

No guard, lock, or ordering prevents this; the resolver simply has an inconsistent (missing) transport gate relative to its two collaborators, leaving an orphan bean + a misleading WARN + a config (offline.enable=true) that quietly does nothing.

SEVERITY: MINOR is correct and the finding is honestly bounded — it explicitly concedes no NPE, no crash, and that spec §9 scopes RC2 to Redis-only so the inert behavior is in-scope/documented. It is an ergonomics/consistency defect (orphan bean, misleading log, silent no-op on an explicitly-enabled flag), not a functional break. Note on the regression lens: single-node and RC1 behavior are unchanged (offline beans don't exist there at all), so this is a new-feature gating inconsistency rather than a behavioral regression of a prior release — but the defect is demonstrably real in this actual code, so it survives refutation at MINOR.

mustFixBeforeCut.title: delete() unconditionally DELs the drain lock without ownership check (Redlock release-foreign-lock)
mustFixBeforeCut.touchesMustFix: multi-device exactly-once (degrades, does not unconditionally break — gated behind a >drainLockMs timing window; no permanent loss; spec already scopes feature as at-least-once/idempotent)
mustFixBeforeCut.fix: Make release a compare-and-DEL: Lua `if redis.call('get',KEYS[1])==ARGV[1] then return redis.call('del',KEYS[1]) end` (or GETDEL-guard) keyed on nodeId, so delete() only removes the lock it still owns; do NOT DEL a foreign device's freshly-acquired lock.
mustFixBeforeCut.reason: Only confirmed finding touching a headline must-fix (multi-device dedup). Not a cut blocker because it self-heals, causes no permanent loss, and is gated behind a timing precondition — but it is the highest-value pre-final-cut hardening and its probability is amplified by the unwired drain-batch-size below.

backlog.title: drain-batch-size documented + bound + defaulted (100) but wired NOWHERE — drain() XRANGE Range.unbounded() reads/delivers the whole stream
backlog.fix: Thread o.getDrainBatchSize() into the RedisOfflineQueueStore constructor and use the bounded XRANGE form `xrange(key, Range.unbounded(), Limit.from(drainBatchSize))`; deliver+ack only that batch and leave the rest for the next drain (and re-drain via a short re-trigger or rely on subsequent connect). ALTERNATIVELY, if per-connect pacing is out of RC2 scope, remove the knob + the three doc promises (spec §7 L203, api-guide.md:814/1083, release-notes-1.10.0.md:222/425, metadata.json:216-219). Confirmed zero call sites of getDrainBatchSize() in main code. Note: also raises the probability of the FOREIGN-LOCK-DEL race by allowing an unbounded (up to max-messages-per-user=1000) synchronous deliver loop to exceed drainLockMs.
backlog.rationale: Over-delivery / dead documented config knob, not loss/security/disabled-path. Confirmed MAJOR on the cluster lens (broken documented contract + inert config) but does not break the three must-fixes or the byte-identical-disabled contract.

backlog.title: netty.cluster.offline.dropped_retention meter registered but addOfflineDroppedRetention() has zero callers — the 'bounded-gap honesty meter' is pinned at 0
backlog.file: ClusterRuntimeStats.java:146 (mutator, no callers); RedisOfflineQueueStore.java:121 (MAXLEN trim, server-side, uncountable) + 153-158 (TTL drop, no bump); NettyClusterMeterBinder.java:122-124
backlog.fix: Increment offlineDroppedRetention on the lazy TTL drop in drain() (pass a ClusterRuntimeStats ref into RedisOfflineQueueStore, bump once per TTL-skipped entry). MAXLEN ~ server-side eviction is genuinely uncountable from the app — either soften the spec/release-notes claim for the MAXLEN path (document it as TTL-only metering) or approximate via XLEN-before/after. At minimum the documented operator alert `rate(...dropped_retention[5m]) > 0` must be able to fire for the TTL path. Verified: incOfflineEnqueued/addOfflineDrained ARE wired, so this meter is an inconsistent gap, not a pattern.
backlog.rationale: Observability/honesty-contract gap on the loss path; the trim itself is by-design bounded loss (spec-honest). No crash/corruption/silent-message-loss. MAJOR doc-honesty, not a must-fix breach.

backlog.title: Drain lock leaks on every empty-queue (and TTL/HMAC-only) connect — held until PX (~5s) expiry; can delay backfill on a concurrent device reconnect
backlog.file: ClusterSessionHookImpl.java:142-143 (early return, no delete()); RedisOfflineQueueStore.java:144-146 (drain returns empty while still holding lock); release only in delete():191
backlog.fix: Release the lock on the empty / all-skipped return path — either (a) drain() DELs its own lock before returning Collections.emptyList() (only when it actually acquired it this call, not when SET NX failed), or (b) the caller always calls a release/ack even when messages is empty. Preferred: pair acquire/release inside drain() with a try/finally, and have delete() only XDEL (no lock DEL). Verified self-heals via PX (default 5000ms) so no permanent loss, but it churns the lock on the common empty-connect path and can delay a concurrent-device backfill by up to drainLockMs.
backlog.rationale: Self-healing via PX expiry; message stays in stream and redelivers on a later reconnect — at-least-once preserved. Concurrency/lock-lifecycle defect, not permanent loss. Bundle with FOREIGN-LOCK-DEL since both are the same drain-lock lifecycle.

backlog.title: TTL-expired / HMAC-rejected / decode-failed entries are skip-via-continue, never added to delivered ids, hence never XDEL'd — re-read every drain forever; the inline comment claiming they are XDEL'd 'alongside the delivered ids' is factually false
backlog.file: RedisOfflineQueueStore.java:151-177 (skip paths + false comment) vs ClusterSessionHookImpl.java:145-153 (deliveredIds built only from returned messages)
backlog.fix: Collect skipped (TTL/HMAC/decode-fail) ids into a separate list and XDEL them too (or have drain() XDEL them inline), so poison/expired entries are reaped instead of re-decoded on every connect. At minimum, correct the false inline comment at L151-152. Bounded by MAXLEN ~ max-messages-per-user so not an unbounded leak, but a per-connect re-decode waste + provably-wrong comment.
backlog.rationale: Bounded by MAXLEN; no delivery-correctness impact (valid entries still decode+deliver). Cleanliness/efficiency + false-comment. MINOR.

backlog.title: Offline stream key has no Redis EXPIRE/PEXPIRE — a user enqueued-to but who never reconnects leaks the key permanently (TTL is lazy-checked on drain only)
backlog.fix: Add a key-level PEXPIRE on enqueue (e.g. PEXPIRE = ttlMillis, refreshed per XADD) so abandoned/never-reconnecting-user streams self-reap. One-line `.pexpire()`. Spec is internally consistent (ttl-seconds documented as lazy-on-drain), so this is a design-gap closure for the never-reconnect case, not a code-vs-spec break. Opt-in path + MAXLEN bounds per-key size, so key-COUNT growth only.
backlog.rationale: Opt-in (default off), per-key size bounded by MAXLEN, slow operational key-count growth only. No loss/corruption/security. MINOR.

backlog.title: userIdResolver bean lacks the STANDALONE_REDIS_REGISTRY gate that userRegistry+offlineQueueStore carry — on Redis-Cluster/all-NATS transports with offline.enable=true the resolver is created (with its loud 'CONVENIENCE/TESTING ONLY' WARN) while registry+store are not, so offline silently no-ops with an orphan bean
backlog.fix: Add @ConditionalOnExpression(STANDALONE_REDIS_REGISTRY) to the userIdResolver @Bean (matching its two collaborators), OR fail-fast when offline.enable=true on a non-standalone transport. Spec §9 scopes RC2 to Redis-only so the inert behavior is in-scope; this is gating consistency. No NPE/crash; single-node + RC1 paths unchanged (offline beans don't exist there).
backlog.rationale: Ergonomics/consistency: orphan bean + misleading WARN + silent no-op of an explicitly-enabled flag on an out-of-RC2-scope transport. Disabled-path and prior-release behavior unchanged. MINOR.

synthesis.summary: RC2 IS READY TO CUT. Zero confirmed BLOCKER and zero confirmed MAJOR that breaks the three must-fixes (security / multi-device exactly-once / no-silent-loss) or the offline.enable=false byte-identical contract. I re-verified every load-bearing fact directly against the branch code. Security lens is fully clean. The offline.enable=false byte-identical contract is INTACT: ClusterSessionHookImpl forces offlineEnabled=false unless all collaborators are non-null (L84), guards the resolver call (L98), and passes Collections.emptyMap() exactly as RC1 (L127); the registry/store beans are @ConditionalOnProperty(offline.enable=true), so the disabled path is untouched by every confirmed finding. No-silent-loss is INTACT: every loss-adjacent finding (empty-drain lock leak, foreign-lock DEL, TTL/HMAC non-XDEL, missing stream EXPIRE) was confirmed to NOT cause permanent loss — messages stay in the Redis stream and redeliver on a later reconnect (the drain lock self-heals via PX expiry), and bounded MAXLEN/TTL trim is by-design at-least-once-within-retention, which the spec explicitly documents. Multi-device exactly-once: the spec itself disclaims exactly-once (spec §6 + release-notes: 'Not exactly-once, handlers must be idempotent, at-least-once'); the headline guarantee is only that the per-userId drain lock kills DETERMINISTIC double-delivery. The one MAJOR that touches that guarantee — delete()'s unconditional foreign-lock DEL (RedisOfflineQueueStore.java:191) — degrades the lock only under a timing window (drain+deliver exceeding drainLockMs concurrent with a second-device reconnect) without permanent loss, so it does not unconditionally break the must-fix; per the stated rule it goes to mustFixBeforeCut as the top pre-final-cut hardening rather than a cut blocker. Its probability is amplified by the unwired drain-batch-size (drain reads the whole stream unbounded up to 1000), so fixing the batch knob and the lock together is the right RC2-followup bundle. Backlog (cut proceeds): 3 MAJOR doc-honesty/robustness items (inert drain-batch-size knob; dropped_retention meter pinned at 0; empty-drain lock leak) and 3 MINOR items (poison/TTL entries never XDEL'd + false inline comment; no key-level stream EXPIRE; orphan userIdResolver bean + misleading WARN on non-standalone transports). None break security, the disabled-path contract, or no-silent-loss. Note: the foreign-lock-DEL finding's claim that the project 'already does check-and-del for its other SET-NX locks' is FALSE (RedisClusterReaper relies on PX expiry, never explicitly releases) — this mischaracterizes precedent but does not undermine the defect; GETDEL/Lua compare-and-DEL fencing is still the correct fix.
